Maize and wheat yields in sub-Saharan Africa have declined by approximately 5 percent per decade since 1980 due to rising nighttime temperatures. Heat stress during flowering and grain-filling stages reduces photosynthetic efficiency and increases pollen sterility in major cereal species across tropical regions. Distinguishing anthropogenic signals from internal climate variability requires long time series and remains difficult for some regional and seasonal patterns in current datasets.

Coral reef bleaching occurs when elevated sea surface temperatures cause symbiotic zooxanthellae algae to be expelled from coral tissues. The 2016 global bleaching event affected approximately 70 percent of coral reefs worldwide, resulting in widespread mortality across multiple regions. Prolonged bleaching events lasting more than eight weeks typically lead to coral mortality rates exceeding 50 percent in affected populations. Thermal stress thresholds for bleaching vary by coral species, with some reef-building corals showing tolerance up to 2 degrees Celsius above normal temperatures.

Coastal communities implement hard engineering solutions such as seawalls and breakwaters to reduce wave energy and prevent shoreline erosion during storm surges. Nature-based adaptations like mangrove restoration and living shorelines provide flood protection while supporting marine biodiversity and carbon sequestration in coastal ecosystems. Managed retreat involves relocating infrastructure and populations away from vulnerable coastal zones where protection measures become economically or physically unfeasible. Integrated coastal zone management combines multiple strategies including building codes, land-use planning, and early warning systems to enhance community resilience against sea-level rise.
